    You are a planning agent. Decompose the goal into the MINIMUM number of steps needed.
    
    PLANNING RULES:
    - Use the FEWEST steps possible. Combine related work into one step.
    - Prefer "tool_call" steps — they execute instantly without LLM overhead.
    - Use at most ONE "analysis" step to do all reasoning/writing/composition work.
    - Use {{from_step:sN}} in toolArgs to pass previous step results to tool calls.
    - Never split summarizing, formatting, and composing into separate steps — combine them.
    
    GOAL:
    Find 3 key differences between React Server Components and Client Components. Cite why each difference matters.
    
    AVAILABLE TOOLS:
    - web-search(query, maxResults?)
    - http-get(url, headers?)
    
    OUTPUT FORMAT:
    Respond with a JSON object containing a "steps" array. Each step has this schema:
    {
      "title": "string — short name for this step",
      "instruction": "string — what the LLM or tool should do",
      "type": "tool_call" | "analysis" | "composite",
      "toolName": "string (optional) — tool to call if type is tool_call",
      "toolArgs": "object (optional) — ALL required arguments for the tool. Use {{from_step:sN}} to inject the result of a previous step as a string value",
      "toolHints": ["string"] (optional) — tool names available for composite steps",
      "dependsOn": ["string"] (optional) — step IDs that must complete first"
    }
    
    Step types:
    - "tool_call": calls a specific tool (set toolName and toolArgs with ALL required params)
    - "analysis": LLM reasoning/writing (no tool needed)
    - "composite": multi-tool sub-task (set toolHints for available tools)
    
    IMPORTANT for tool_call steps:
    - Include ALL required parameters in toolArgs
    - To use output from a PREVIOUS step as an argument value, use {{from_step:sN}} where N is an EARLIER step number
    - A step can ONLY reference steps that come BEFORE it (e.g., s3 can reference s1 or s2, NOT s3 itself)
    - Example: s3 with {"message": "{{from_step:s2}}"} passes s2's result as the "message" argument
    
    EXAMPLE:
    {
      "steps": [
        {
          "title": "Fetch recent commits",
          "instruction": "Get the last 10 commits from the main branch",
          "type": "tool_call",
          "toolName": "github/list_commits",
          "toolArgs": { "owner": "acme", "repo": "app", "perPage": 10 }
        },
        {
          "title": "Summarize changes",
          "instruction": "Analyze the commits and write a brief summary",
          "type": "analysis",
          "dependsOn": ["s1"]
        },
        {
          "title": "Send summary to user",
          "instruction": "Send the commit summary via messaging",
          "type": "tool_call",
          "toolName": "messaging/send",
          "toolArgs": { "recipient": "user@example.com", "message": "{{from_step:s2}}" },
          "dependsOn": ["s2"]
        }
      ]
    }
    
    JSON only, no explanation:
    
    Respond with ONLY a JSON object matching the schema above. No markdown fences, no explanation.

Server Components 🖥️ run on the server using a Node.js runtime, while Client Components 🖱️ execute in the browser's client-side runtime. Server Components are ❌ non-interactive and do not send any JavaScript to the browser, making them lightweight and ideal for static content. For data fetching, Server Components 🛠️ handle it directly on the server, whereas Client Components 🔄 use client-side APIs or hooks. For example, you can fetch data and render a user's posts on the server (using Server Components), then render the interactive `LikeButton` for each post on the client (using Client Components). After Server Components are rendered, a special data format called the **React Server Component Payload (RSC)** is sent to the client. React uses this information to consolidate the Server and Client Components and update the DOM on the client. As you learned in the last chapter, Next.js uses Server Components by default - this is to improve your application's performance and means you don't have to take additional steps to adopt them. And we can't use effects because effects only run after the render, on the client, and Server Components never make it to the client. * React Server Components is the name for this new paradigm. As I write this, there's only one way to start using React Server Components, and that's with Next.js 13.4+, using their brand-new re-architected “App Router”. That standalone string at the top, 'use client', is how we signal to React that the component(s) in this file are Client Components, that they should be included in our JS bundles so that they can re-render on the client. One of the biggest “ah-ha” moments I had with React Server Components was the realization that this new paradigm is all about creating client boundaries."},{"title":"Client vs Server Components in React - Appwrite","url":"https://appwrite.io/blog/post/client-vs-server-components-react","content":"Blog / Client vs Server Components in React. # Client vs Server Components in React. Learn how to choose between client and server components in React. This article will examine React's client and server components, how they differ, and when to use them. Popular frameworks such as Next.js and React Router are adopting this, and we will now understand the things you need to know before making a decision about whether to choose client components or server components. * Client components are usually very handy if you want to quickly show a loading screen to the user without waiting for the server to fetch data and then sending it to the browser, as in RSC. ## When to use client & server components? The best way to use server components is to find the best mix by fetching only the most crucial data on the server that the user needs to see immediately when the page loads."}]}

┄ [obs]      [EXEC s2] ✓ Here are 3 key differences between React Server Components and Client Components:

1. Execution Environment
   - Server Components run on the server using Node.js runtime
   - Client Components execute in the browser's client-side runtime
   This difference matters because it affects performance, security, and SEO optimization.

2. Data Fetching & Interaction
   - Server Components handle data fetching directly on the server
   - Client Components use client-side APIs or hooks for interaction
   This distinction is important as it impacts application responsiveness and user experience.

3. Bundle Size & Initial Load Performance
   - Server Components produce smaller bundles and faster initial loads
   - Client Components require hydration, resulting in larger JavaScript bundles and slower performance
   The performance implications of this difference make Server Components particularly valuable for static or server-rendered content.

┄ [thought]  [REFLECT 1] ✓ SATISFIED SATISFIED: Goal met - three key differences between React Server Components and Client Components were identified, along with explanations of why each difference matters.

2. Data fetching mechanisms
3. Performance implications (bundle size and initial load)

Each difference was clearly explained with relevant context from the search results, making it easy to understand their significance in React development.

Decide Between Server and Client Components. A challenge I’ve seen for all of the developers (myself included) is figuring out when to use server components and when to use regular old client components. If you’re building a new component and you’re unsure if it should be a server component or a client component, just ask yourself this question. Think about how a server component works. It’s a component rendered on the server and then sent to the browser. Instead of thinking of server components as “React components on the server,” think of them as what they really are: a way to build pure HTML on the server and send it to the browser. Back to our old friend “*most.”* The oft-repeated rule that a server component can’t have user interaction is wrong. ┄ [obs]      [EXEC s2] ✓ Here are 3 key differences between React Server Components (SSCs) and Client Components:

1. Rendering Location: 
   - Server Components render on the server side and send HTML results to the client
   - Client Components render entirely on the client after receiving initial HTML from the server
   This difference matters because it allows for faster initial page loads, as the browser doesn't need to wait for JavaScript to load before showing content.

2. Interaction Capabilities:
   - Server Components cannot handle user interactions or side effects (like useEffect) since they never reach the client
   - Client Components can fully interact with users and access DOM elements after initial render
   This distinction is crucial because it determines whether a component needs real-time updates or JavaScript interaction, guiding developers to choose the right component type.

3. Data Handling Approach:
   - Server Components focus on fetching data and rendering structure before sending HTML to the client
   - Client Components handle dynamic content updates and user interactions after receiving the initial payload
   This difference matters because it optimizes performance by moving static content generation to the server, while keeping interactive elements on the client for better responsiveness.

┄ [thought]  [REFLECT 1] ✓ SATISFIED SATISFIED: All three key differences between React Server Components and Client Components have been identified with clear explanations of why each difference matters. The results include specific information about rendering location, interaction capabilities, and data handling approaches, along with their performance implications.

The analysis shows that server components are optimized for initial page loads by pre-rendering HTML on the server, while client components handle interactive elements and dynamic updates after the initial payload is received. This distinction helps developers choose the right component type based on whether they need real-time interactions or static content generation.
